items.title: POP-Q classification
items.contentHtml:
POP-Q Classification

  • The Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification (POP-Q) system uses six fixed points (Aa, Ba, C, D, Ap, Bp) referred to the hymen as a reference plane (value 0).
  • The anterior and posterior points are measured in centimeters proximal (negative) or distal (positive) to the hymen; Point C represents the cervix or vaginal vault.
  • The distance genital hiatus (Gh), perineal body (Pb) and vaginal total length (Tvl) complement the classification.
  • It is mandatory to report the stage and the most descended point to plan the surgery.

items.title: POP-Q stadiums
items.contentHtml:
Prolapse stages according to POP-Q

  • Stage 0: all points are > -1 cm from the hymen (without prolapse).
  • Stage I: the most descended point is > -1 cm but < +1 cm from the hymen.
  • Stage II: the most descended point is between -1 cm and +1 cm (inside the vaginal canal).
  • Stage III: the most descended point protrudes > +1 cm but < (Tvl - 2) cm.
  • Stage IV: complete eversion of the vagina; the most descended point is at > (Tvl - 2) cm.
  • The stage is defined by the point with the greatest descent, not by the compartment.

items.title: Apical symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Symptoms of apical prolapse

  • Apical prolapse (descent of the cervix or vaginal vault) produces a sensation of ball or vaginal pressure, which worsens with standing and straining.
  • It can be associated with dyspareunia, lower lumbar pain and a sensation of incomplete emptying.
  • Prolapse Advanced apical incontinence (stage III-IV) can cause urinary retention due to urethral kinking and flow obstruction.
  • Stress urinary incontinence can be hidden (occult prolapse) and manifest after surgical correction.

items.title: Vaginal birth as a risk
items.contentHtml:
Risk factor: vaginal birth

  • Vaginal birth is the main risk factor for pelvic organ prolapse, due to injury to the levator ani complex (muscle avulsion) and the tendinous arch of the pelvic fascia.
  • The risk increases with the number of vaginal births (parity ≥ 2), fetal weight > 4000 g, use of forceps and third or fourth degree perineal tears.
  • Elective cesarean section prior to active labor is associated with a lower risk of prolapse than vaginal delivery.
  • obesity and chronic constipation act as cofactors that increase intra-abdominal pressure.

items.title: Differential Dx with IU
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with urinary incontinence

  • Pelvic organ prolapse can coexist with stress urinary incontinence (SUI), but also simulate it: voiding urgency and urge incontinence are common due to urethral hypermobility or obstruction.
  • occult stress urinary incontinence is diagnosed after reducing the prolapse with a pessary or speculum (prolapse reduction test).
  • incomplete emptying (postvoid residual > 100 mL) points to obstructive prolapse, not SUI. pure.
  • Vinting cystourethrography or dynamic ultrasound differentiate the descent of the anterior wall from intrinsic incontinence.

items.title: Pessaries as treatment
items.contentHtml:
Conservative treatment: pessaries

  • Vaginal pessaries (ring, cube, Gellhorn type) are the first line of conservative treatment in women with symptomatic prolapse who wish to avoid surgery or have contraindications.
  • They are indicated in stages II-IV; success is assessed with the pessary test (insertion and removal at 2-4 weeks). Complications include vaginal erosion, malodorous discharge, and urinary retention; They require periodic removal and cleaning (every 2-3 months).
  • The ring pessary is the most used due to its easy insertion and removal.

items.title: Surgical indications
items.contentHtml:
Indications for surgery for prolapse

  • Surgery is indicated when the prolapse is symptomatic (sensation of lump, pain, voiding/defecatory dysfunction) and conservative treatment has failed or is not desired.
  • It is considered in stages III-IV of the Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification (POP-Q) system or in stage II with severe symptoms.
  • Urinary obstruction with post-void residue > 150 mL or hydronephrosis is an absolute indication.
  • In asymptomatic women or with mild prolapse (stage I-II), surgery is not recommended, unless there is reproductive desire or complications.

items.title: Anterior vs posterior repair
items.contentHtml:
Anterior vs posterior repair

  • The anterior repair (anterior colporrhaphy) corrects the prolapse of the anterior vaginal wall (cystocele) by plication of the pubocervical fascia.
  • The posterior repair (posterior colporrhaphy) corrects the prolapse of the posterior vaginal wall (rectocele) plicating the rectovaginal fascia.
  • Posterior repair can be associated with plication of the levator ani to correct the perineal defect, but increases the risk of dyspareunia.
  • Both repairs can be combined with sacrocolpopexy if there is concomitant apical prolapse.

items.title: Standard sacrocolpopexy
items.contentHtml:
Sacrocolpopexy as a standard technique

  • Sacrocolpopexy (open or laparoscopic/robotic) is the reference treatment for apical prolapse (vaginal dome or cervix) in stages III-IV.
  • It consists of fixing the vaginal vault to the sacral promontory using a graft of polypropylene mesh (type I, macropore).
  • The laparoscopic approach offers less morbidity and faster recovery than the open abdominal approach, with anatomical success rates > 90%.
  • Contraindications: active pelvic infection, desire for future fertility (relative) and active inflammatory bowel disease.

items.title: Mesh complications
items.contentHtml:
Surgical mesh complications

  • Specific complications of polypropylene mesh include vaginal erosion/exposure (2-10%), chronic infection, pelvic/de novo pain and dyspareunia.
  • mesh erosion manifests as vaginal bleeding, discharge purulent or dyspareunia; the diagnosis is clinical and confirmed with speculoscopy.
  • Treatment of erosion includes topical estrogenization and, if it persists, partial or total surgical resection.
  • mesh retraction can cause ureteral obstruction or fistula; type I mesh (macropore) reduces the risk of infection.

items.title: Association with fecal incontinence
items.contentHtml:
Association with fecal incontinence

  • Pelvic organ prolapse is associated with fecal incontinence due to injury to the external and internal anal sphincter (vaginal delivery) and descent of the rectum (rectocele).
  • fecal urge incontinence (incapacity) to delay defecation) is more common than passive defecation in women with posterior prolapse.
  • Anorectal manometry and endoanal ultrasound differentiate sphincter injury from rectal prolapse.
  • Combined treatment (prolapse repair and pelvic floor rehabilitation) improves fecal continence in up to 70% of cases.

items.title: Postmenopausal management
items.contentHtml:
Management in postmenopausal women

  • In postmenopausal women with symptomatic prolapse, local hormone therapy (vaginal estrogens in cream, ring, or tablet) improves the quality of vaginal tissue and reduces the risk of pessary or mesh erosion.
  • Systemic hormone therapy is not recommended to treat prolapse; only the topical one has proven benefit.
  • Postmenopausal vaginal atrophy can exacerbate prolapse symptoms (dryness, dyspareunia) and should be treated before any surgery.
  • In women without contraindications, the vaginal pessary is a first-line conservative option, always with the application of topical estrogens to prevent erosions.
